title: Physical and Logical Security, Identity, Authentication, and Access · Evidence Workshop
summary: A guided support bench extends identity and least-privilege access model while retaining authorization, safety, baseline, privacy, accessibility, rollback, verification, documentation, and user communication. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of identity and least-privilege access model while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Classify physical, technical, administrative, preventive, deterrent, detective, corrective, compensating, and directive controls by threat and evidence.
Separate person, device, workload, account, identifier, credential, authenticator, authentication, authorization, session, role, group, and audit event.
Compare knowledge, possession, inherence, location, behavior, certificates, tokens, biometrics, MFA, passwordless, fallback, recovery, and enrollment risks.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# comptia-a-plus cumulative fictional support evidence portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic text-contract review only. They never execute learner
# commands, inspect the host, touch devices or accounts, connect to networks, open equipment,
# handle malware, erase media, contact people, or claim certification.
workspace: support
ticket-id: fictional-ticket
user-impact: [replace with user outcome, scope, severity, and urgency]
environment: [replace with asset, version, configuration, owner, dependency, and evidence time]
safety-authority: [replace with consent, hazards, data custody, privacy, access, and stop rules]
baseline: [replace with expected behavior, known-good state, conditions, and measurement]
symptom: [replace with observed behavior, reproduction, frequency, timing, and evidence]
recent-change: [replace with timeline, relevant changes, counterevidence, and unknowns]
hypothesis: [replace with falsifiable cause, competing explanation, prediction, and confidence]
test: [replace with one safe reversible discriminating test, changed case, and stop rule]
observation: [replace with actual result, provenance, rejected inference, and next decision]
repair-rollback: [replace with least-change repair, authority, backup, trigger, and restored state]
verify: [replace with user outcome, original, changed, side-effect, restart, and recurrence checks]
prevention: [replace with prevention, monitoring, owner, review date, and residual risk]
escalation: [replace with stop threshold, destination, evidence packet, and requested decision]
documentation: [replace with plain structured status, redaction, access, handoff, and uncertainty]
transfer-boundary: authorized disposable equipment or simulator only
review:

steps.title: CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Security Control Types
steps.instruction:
workshop case — People, accounts, credentials, devices, roles, badges, sessions, groups, guests, and service identities are conflated after unauthorized access. The workshop variation 2 changes the user outcome, device, version, hazard, recent change, symptom, access need, competing cause, and transfer limit. Competency probe: defend one safe causal support decision

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Security Control Types.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for classify physical, technical, administrative, preventive, deterrent, detective, corrective, compensating, and directive controls by threat and evidence. For CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Security Control Types, keep Security Control Types connected to mechanically reviewable user-impact, asset, safety, baseline, symptom, hypothesis, test, observation, repair, rollback, verification, escalation, documentation, and transfer decision evidence.

content.text: People, accounts, credentials, devices, roles, badges, sessions, groups, guests, and service identities are conflated after unauthorized access. The workshop variation 2 changes the user outcome, device, version, hazard, recent change, symptom, access need, competing cause, and transfer limit. Competency probe: defend one safe causal support decision

content.title: Commit before feedback
content.text: Choose from the stated data before opening the worked model. Record which Security Control Types condition decided your prediction.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: CompTIA A+ Core 1 220-1201 Exam Objectives — Controls every current Core 1 mobile, networking, hardware, virtualization, cloud, and troubleshooting objective and weighting.

hints: For CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Security Control Types, name the user impact, fictional ticket, asset, environment, authorization, hazard, baseline, symptom, timeline, hypothesis, test, observation, repair, rollback, verification, prevention, escalation, accessible documentation, and transfer boundary, Security Control Types precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Security Control Types, trace this concrete input and predict user impact, safety and authority, baseline, reproduction, timeline, hypothesis, bounded test, observation, rejection, repair, rollback, original and changed-case verification, prevention, escalation, and controlled transfer evidence before reviewing it: People, accounts, credentials, devices, roles, badges, sessions, groups, guests, and service identities are conflated after unauthorized access. The workshop variation 2 changes the user outcome, device, version, hazard, recent change, symptom, access need, competing cause, and transfer limit. Competency probe: defend one safe causal support decision

For CompTIA A+ support Commit to the mission model · Workshop Security Control Types, challenge the documented misconception with changed-user, device, version, hazard, baseline, symptom, timeline, access need, hypothesis, test result, repair, restart, recurrence, escalation, and controlled equipment-transfer evidence: One control category is inherently stronger than layered controls with different failure modes.

options.text: For commit to the mission model, apply one familiar repair, omit user impact, authorization, safety, asset and version identity, baseline, recent change, competing cause, prediction, observation, rollback, changed-case verification, accessibility, privacy, escalation, and controlled transfer, then accept a missing symptom as support proof. This does not satisfy Security Control Types for identity and least-privilege access model.
State the Security Control Types preconditions, preserve earlier contracts, and name the trace or changed-input result that will decide success.
One control category is inherently stronger than layered controls with different failure modes. That claim fails the commit to the mission model evidence boundary for Security Control Types.
